purpose: RAG injection to improve Gemini translation accuracy on Turkana voice recordings

sentence_structure.example: alosi ayong moi locor = I will go tomorrow to the well
sentence_structure.note: Element can be fronted for emphasis. Front element = stressed information.
stress_examples: moi alosi ayong locor = TOMORROW I will go to the well
locor alosi ayong moi = It is to THE WELL I will go tomorrow
sentence_structure.emergency_implication: If a word appears FIRST in a Turkana sentence, it is the most urgent information.

verb_classes.note: CRITICAL for translation accuracy. Wrong class = wrong meaning.
verb_classes.causative_K: s + vowel prefix (e.g. akisilip = to cause to beg)
verb_classes.causative_T: t + vowel prefix (e.g. akitoper = to cause to rest)

tenses.future_marker: + moi (e.g. elipi moi = I will pray)
tenses.negative_prefix: ny- (e.g. nyelipi = I am not praying)
tenses.negative_alternative: pa + verb (e.g. pa alosi = I am not going)
tenses.emergency_note: CRITICAL: ny- prefix negates the entire action. Missing this = catastrophic mistranslation. 'nyepi' does NOT mean eating — it means NOT eating.

emergency_vocabulary.urgent_help: kilip = imperative: YOU beg/ask for help NOW
emergency_vocabulary.rest_sleep: akiper (T.O.) — used in medical context: patient needs to rest
emergency_vocabulary.herd_move: akiyok (K.O.) — also implies moving/relocating animals or people

variants: Kakuma area: slight phonological variations in vowel length
Lokichoggio corridor: closer to Topossa influence — foreign people names are masculine, their languages feminine
Karamoja variant: documented by Hulley 1923 — shared roots with Karamoja and Topossa
North Turkana vs South Turkana: tonal register differences affect verb class identification
dialect_notes.code_switching: Speakers in Kakuma refugee camp often mix Turkana + Kiswahili + English. Treat each word independently for keyword detection.

translation_validation_rules.check: If ny- prefix appears, translation MUST contain 'not' or 'no' or 'without'
translation_validation_rules.example: nyapei ≠ there is water — it means THERE IS NO water

translation_validation_rules.check: First word in sentence = most stressed. If it is an emergency word, flag URGENT
ngakipi is plural feminine — 'some water' not 'a water'
kilip / kiyok / tacap / toper = imperative commands (DO THIS NOW). Flag as urgent.

gemini_injection_prompt: You are translating a voice recording from Turkana (Ngaturkana). Apply these linguistic rules: (1) Turkana sentence order is VSO — the FIRST word is the most stressed/urgent element. (2) The prefix ny- or pa+ NEGATES the verb — 'nyapei' means NO WATER not 'there is water'. (3) Imperative forms kilip/kiyok/tacap/toper mean DO THIS NOW — flag as urgent. (4) ngakipi = water (plural feminine). ikoku/ngide = child/children. (5) Code-switching between Turkana, Kiswahili, and English is common in Kakuma — translate each language segment independently.
